article_meta.title: The three-way battle between Democrats running for Senate in Illinois has evolved into an argument about the best way to

transcript.content:
You are a news summarization assistant. Summarize the following article and extract its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "summary": "string (150-200 words)",
  "key_facts": ["string","string","string","string","string"],
  "sources_represented": ["string"]
}
Rules:
- summary must be 150-200 words
- key_facts must contain EXACTLY 5 items
- sources_represented must list every distinct perspective included

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: The three-way battle between Democrats running for Senate in Illinois has evolved into an argument about the best way to
SOURCE: New York Times (News)

ARTICLE:

The three-way battle between Democrats running for Senate in Illinois has evolved into an argument about the best way to combat President Trump’s deportation agenda.Is it better to “abolish ICE,” as Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton offers, or to “abolish Trump’s ICE,” as Representative Raja Krishnamoorthi promises?The contest’s third candidate, Representative Robin Kelly, said the country could not just abolish U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement without a plan to replace the agency’s border enforcement function, but she introduced legislation last month to impeach Kristi Noem, the Homeland Security secretary — a bill Mr. Krishnamoorthi quickly signed onto.The tenor of the Illinois race had a focus on immigration when the Trump administration sent federal agents to Chicago last fall and then shifted fully to deportation policy after Border Patrol agents shot and killed Alex Pretti in Minneapolis. The three Senate candidates, who aren’t split by major ideological or policy differences, now offer a window into how Democrats could approach dismantling Mr. Trump’s immigration enforcement structure if they win back full control in Washington.Early voting has begun for the March 17 primary race, and there is evidence in other contests that a strong anti-ICE agenda can work in low-turnout Democratic primaries. Last week in New Jersey, Analilia Mejia won a 13-candidate special primary election running as the most progressive candidate in the field on a pledge to abolish ICE. Democratic anger over Mr. Trump’s deportation policies led to a shutdown of the Department of Homeland Security when its funding expired this weekend.And yet ambitious Democrats who saw how calls to “abolish ICE” and “defund the police” hurt the party in recent years are concerned that running on a definitive message to abolish the agency is risky politics — if not in 2026 then beyond.We are having trouble retrieving the article content.Please enable JavaScript in your browser settings.Thank you for your patience while we verify access. If you are in Reader mode please exit and log into your Times account, or subscribe for all of The Times.Thank you for your patience while we verify access.Already a subscriber? Log in.Want all of The Times? Subscribe.
---

```json
{
  "summary": "The Democratic primary race for an Illinois Senate seat has centered on immigration policy and how to counter President Trump's deportation agenda. Three candidates—Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton, Rep. Raja Krishnamoorthi, and Rep. Robin Kelly—are competing with varying approaches to Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE). Stratton advocates abolishing ICE entirely, while Krishnamoorthi proposes abolishing \"Trump's ICE.\" Kelly argues against abolishing ICE without a replacement plan but has introduced legislation to impeach Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem. The race intensified after federal agents were sent to Chicago and Border Patrol agents killed Alex Pretti in Minneapolis. Early voting began March 17, with evidence from other races, like Analilia Mejia's New Jersey primary win, suggesting anti-ICE positions can succeed in low-turnout Democratic primaries. However, some Democrats worry that abolishing ICE could become politically risky, given past backlash to progressive slogans like \"defund the police.\"",
  "key_facts": [
    "Three Democrats—Juliana Stratton, Raja Krishnamoorthi, and Robin Kelly—are competing in Illinois's Senate primary with differing stances on ICE and immigration enforcement",
    "Stratton calls for abolishing ICE, Krishnamoorthi proposes abolishing \"Trump's ICE,\" and Kelly opposes abolition without a replacement plan",
    "The race's focus on immigration intensified after federal agents were deployed to Chicago and Border Patrol killed Alex Pretti in Minneapolis",
    "Analilia Mejia won a New Jersey special primary on an abolish-ICE platform, suggesting the position can mobilize Democratic voters",
    "Some Democrats fear that calls to abolish ICE could prove politically risky beyond 2026, citing concerns over progressive slogans hurting the party previously"
  ],
  "sources_represented": [
    "New York Times (News)"
  ]
}
```

parsed_output.summary: The Democratic primary race for an Illinois Senate seat has centered on immigration policy and how to counter President Trump's deportation agenda. Three candidates—Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton, Rep. Raja Krishnamoorthi, and Rep. Robin Kelly—are competing with varying approaches to Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE). Stratton advocates abolishing ICE entirely, while Krishnamoorthi proposes abolishing "Trump's ICE." Kelly argues against abolishing ICE without a replacement plan but has introduced legislation to impeach Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem. The race intensified after federal agents were sent to Chicago and Border Patrol agents killed Alex Pretti in Minneapolis. Early voting began March 17, with evidence from other races, like Analilia Mejia's New Jersey primary win, suggesting anti-ICE positions can succeed in low-turnout Democratic primaries. However, some Democrats worry that abolishing ICE could become politically risky, given past backlash to progressive slogans like "defund the police."
key_facts: Three Democrats—Juliana Stratton, Raja Krishnamoorthi, and Robin Kelly—are competing in Illinois's Senate primary with differing stances on ICE and immigration enforcement
Stratton calls for abolishing ICE, Krishnamoorthi proposes abolishing "Trump's ICE," and Kelly opposes abolition without a replacement plan
The race's focus on immigration intensified after federal agents were deployed to Chicago and Border Patrol killed Alex Pretti in Minneapolis
Analilia Mejia won a New Jersey special primary on an abolish-ICE platform, suggesting the position can mobilize Democratic voters
Some Democrats fear that calls to abolish ICE could prove politically risky beyond 2026, citing concerns over progressive slogans hurting the party previously
